Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty intense but also inconsistent in terms of when I actually showed up to things. I'd dedicate around 35 hours a week to studying, but a lot of that was at home with my own materials spread out around me. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create diagrams, color-coded notes, and mind maps that helped me process information better than just reading through slides. The anxiety I deal with made it hard to stick to a perfect schedule, so I'd often study in long bursts when I felt capable rather than following a strict timetable.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on how I was feeling mentally and what deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying the same times each day, but then anxiety would hit and I'd struggle to even start. When deadlines approached, I'd shift into overdrive and study intensely, but that wasn't sustainable. I tried to build regular habits, but my low resilience meant that any disruption would throw me off completely for days.
Q3: Usually I'd log into the LMS when I needed to check if there were new announcements or upcoming deadlines I might have missed. Given my attendance was pretty low, the LMS became my main connection to what was happening in the course. I'd also log in when I felt that surge of anxiety about falling behind, just to reassure myself about where I stood. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch videos when I couldn't face going to actual classes.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS did become somewhat routine for me, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I'd scroll through to see if anything new was posted and check the discussion boards. It became a way to feel connected to the course without the overwhelming pressure of being physically present. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me approach it more calmly rather than with panic.
Q5: The video content was absolutely the most valuable for me – I watched over three hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual information at my own pace. Discussion posts were also helpful because I could engage with peers without the social anxiety of face-to-face interaction. I posted quite a bit in discussions because writing out my thoughts helped me process the material. Visual diagrams and any content with graphics really clicked with how my brain works.
Q6: I tended to ignore the readings that were just walls of text without any visual elements. Live sessions or anything requiring real-time participation I avoided because of my anxiety around synchronous interaction. I also delayed peer collaboration activities – I only had one peer interaction recorded because working with others directly stressed me out significantly. If something didn't have a clear visual component or seemed like it would require spontaneous participation, I'd put it off.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to exam content or when it offered visual explanations of complex concepts. If I could tell it would help reduce my anxiety about understanding the material, I'd invest in it fully. Activities that let me work at my own pace without time pressure felt valuable because I could engage deeply without the stress. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I struggled to prioritize.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. With required work, there was this anxious pressure that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff I'd look at when I was in a good headspace, which wasn't always often given my anxiety levels. Sometimes optional activities were actually more interesting, but the lack of external pressure meant my low motivation made it easy to skip them when things got overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened outside the LMS entirely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work with them offline where I could annotate and create my own visual study guides. I used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp. My physical notebooks are filled with diagrams and mind maps that represent hours of learning the LMS never saw. I also used educational apps and websites that presented information more visually than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend entire afternoons working through downloaded materials, creating elaborate visual summaries, and I'd never touch the LMS during that time. When I was preparing for exams, I'd work almost exclusively from my own reorganized notes and diagrams. Sometimes I'd be studying with a friend who shared their notes, and we'd work through problems together in person. The LMS would show nothing for those days even though I was deeply engaged with the course content.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it immediately, and then all my subsequent engagement happened offline. I'd watch videos multiple times on my laptop without internet, pausing to take visual notes. My downloaded materials became my primary study resources that I'd return to repeatedly over weeks. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt impossible. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd get paralyzed and couldn't make myself do anything productive. My low resilience meant that any setback – a confusing lecture, a poor quiz score – would knock me down for days. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming because I'd see all the things I was behind on.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor because managing high anxiety is exhausting, and some days I just didn't have the energy to engage properly. When I felt confused about course material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because seeing more content I didn't understand increased my anxiety. Competing responsibilities, especially personal stuff, would completely derail my engagement. The mindfulness exercises helped somewhat, but when stress from outside the course piled up, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be scrolling through the LMS, clicking on things, watching videos, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Anxiety would have me physically present but mentally checked out, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd realize I'd watched a twenty-minute video and absorbed nothing because I was too worried about other things. Those sessions felt hollow even though they'd show up as engagement in the logs.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important – my completion rate shows that clearly. The anxiety made starting things incredibly difficult because I'd worry about doing them wrong. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because I was afraid my contributions wouldn't be good enough. Even when I knew procrastinating would make things worse, the short-term relief of avoidance felt necessary for my mental state in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were both a curse and a motivator for me. They'd trigger intense anxiety spikes, but they also forced me to actually engage when nothing else could. I'd often have bursts of LMS activity right before deadlines, cramming in videos and resources I'd been avoiding. Without deadlines, my low motivation meant I'd drift and not engage much at all. The pressure was uncomfortable but sometimes necessary to overcome my avoidance patterns.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost, but negative feedback could send me spiraling and avoiding the course for days. I'd sometimes delay checking feedback because I was anxious about what it might say. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my studying, but vague criticism just increased my anxiety without giving me direction.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat helpful because having clear modules and organized content reduced some of my anxiety about not knowing what to do. However, the pacing sometimes felt too fast for me to keep up, especially with my attendance issues. I appreciated when content was broken into smaller chunks because that felt more manageable. The structure of having both required and optional elements let me prioritize based on my mental capacity on any given day.
Q19: Having video content available made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. The discussion forums were accessible because I could participate asynchronously without real-time pressure. What made things harder was when activities required synchronous participation or group work – those triggered my anxiety significantly. Also, when assignment instructions weren't visually clear or were buried in text, I'd struggle to understand what was expected.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat – I had high engagement with the LMS but didn't complete many assignments. For me, it was the anxiety around actually submitting work and having it judged. I could watch videos and read materials all day, but the act of producing something for evaluation felt paralyzing. Some students might also be exploring and trying to understand without feeling ready to commit their understanding to paper yet.
Q21: That actually resonates with part of my experience too. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and my own visual study systems. Someone could study intensively from physical notes or external resources and barely touch the LMS but still understand the material well. The LMS activity is just one channel of engagement, and for visual learners like me who prefer to work with our own reorganized materials, low LMS activity doesn't mean low learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in – the quantitative stuff. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether someone was actually mentally present or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether the learning actually stuck. For someone like me with high anxiety, the logs might show erratic patterns that look like disengagement when really I was struggling but still trying.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively creating those visual maps and diagrams, when I'm thinking about the content even outside study time. It's about caring whether I actually learn, not just whether I pass. Real engagement involves mental investment and curiosity, which doesn't always translate to clicking buttons on a platform.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my constant internal struggle with anxiety and how much energy it takes just to engage at all. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials creating visual study aids that actually helped me learn. My mindfulness practice and how it affected my ability to approach coursework wouldn't appear anywhere in the logs. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite my struggles with attendance and assignment completion, I genuinely cared about learning and worked hard in my own way.
